blocks.content: On the eighth day he stepped back and looked at what he had made.
It was the largest thing he had ever painted.

Her face. Her warm brown hair — exactly that shade, the one she had worn through all the years he had known her best, the one he had finally gotten right on a wall. The particular expression she wore in an unguarded moment — not a posed face, not a formal portrait. The expression she had when she thought nobody was looking. The one he had memorized. The one he had been drawing, in some form, since the first day.
Behind her, the mountains. The ones she had looked at through the windshield on the first morning of their honeymoon with her whole face open.
Below the portrait, in the careful clean lettering he had practiced on fifty sheets of paper before touching the wall:
In Memory of my beloved wife, Cassandra Carter.
He stood in front of it for a long time.
The fountain behind him. The bench where everything had started.
The afternoon light was exactly the angle he had needed it to be.
